name: Dandelion
short_description: Dandelion is a traditional botanical ingredient used in wellness supplements for digestive, fluid-balance, and general liver-support positioning.
overview_subtitle: A familiar botanical used in teas, capsules, and herbal wellness blends.
overview_content:
Dandelion, from Taraxacum officinale, is used in supplements as root, leaf, or whole-plant extract. The root is commonly associated with digestive and liver-support formulas, while the leaf is often positioned around fluid balance and herbal tea routines.

Dandelion is best understood as a supportive botanical rather than a medical treatment. The plant contains bitter compounds, phenolic acids, and inulin-type fibers depending on the plant part and preparation. Because root and leaf products can differ, supplement labels should clearly state the plant part used.

People with gallbladder conditions, kidney concerns, medication use, or allergies to related plants should seek professional guidance before using dandelion supplements.
interesting_fact: Dandelion root and dandelion leaf are often discussed together, but supplement products may use them for different wellness positioning.
how_it_works_subtitle: Dandelion's activity depends heavily on plant part and preparation.
how_it_works_highlight_title: Root and leaf have different roles
how_it_works_highlight_description: Root extracts are often used in digestion and liver-support formulas, while leaf products are common in fluid-balance tea blends.
how_it_works_content:
Dandelion root contains bitter constituents that can influence digestive secretions and traditional digestive wellness positioning. It may also provide inulin, a type of prebiotic fiber, depending on the preparation.

Dandelion leaf has a different profile and is commonly used in herbal tea products positioned around fluid balance. This distinction matters when comparing supplement claims, because a product labeled simply as dandelion may not clearly explain whether it uses root, leaf, or whole plant.

Dandelion should not be used to self-treat liver, kidney, gallbladder, or fluid-retention concerns. Those situations require professional medical evaluation.
benefits_subtitle: Dandelion is most useful when the plant part and formula purpose are clear.

benefits_json.title: Digestive Wellness Support
benefits_json.description: Dandelion root is traditionally used in bitter botanical formulas connected to digestive support.

benefits_json.title: Fluid-Balance Positioning
benefits_json.description: Dandelion leaf is commonly found in teas and formulas positioned around natural fluid balance.

benefits_json.title: Liver-Support Formulas
benefits_json.description: Dandelion root often appears alongside milk thistle, artichoke, and other liver-support botanicals.

benefits_json.title: Prebiotic Potential
benefits_json.description: Some root preparations contain inulin-type fibers that may support digestive formula positioning.

benefits_json.title: Herbal Tea Use
benefits_json.description: Dandelion is widely used in caffeine-free herbal tea blends and roasted root beverages.

benefits_json.title: Label Comparison Value
benefits_json.description: Dandelion helps shoppers compare plant part, extract format, and combination formulas.

dosage_subtitle: Serving sizes vary by root, leaf, tea, powder, or extract.
typical_dose: Follow label directions; capsules often use hundreds of milligrams per serving, while teas vary by steeping amount.
dosage: Use the serving size listed on the product label and confirm whether the product uses root, leaf, or whole plant.
dosage_content:
Dandelion dosage depends on whether the product is a tea, tincture, powder, capsule, or standardized extract. Root and leaf preparations are not always interchangeable.

People taking medication, managing kidney or gallbladder concerns, or using diuretics should consult a healthcare professional before use.
safety_subtitle: Dandelion is common in herbal products, but it is not appropriate for everyone.

side_effects_json.title: Digestive Sensitivity
side_effects_json.description: Dandelion may cause stomach upset, heartburn, or loose stools in some users.

side_effects_json.title: Allergy Risk
side_effects_json.description: People sensitive to ragweed, daisies, chrysanthemums, or related plants may be more likely to react.

side_effects_json.title: Fluid Balance Effects
side_effects_json.description: Dandelion leaf products may increase urination in some people.

side_effects_json.title: Gallbladder Caution
side_effects_json.description: People with gallstones or bile duct concerns should seek medical guidance before use.

drug_interactions_json: Use caution with diuretics or medications that affect fluid balance.
Discuss use with a clinician if taking lithium or medications affected by hydration changes.
Review with a healthcare professional if using blood pressure medication.
Ask about safety before combining with liver, kidney, or gallbladder-related supplement routines.
who_should_avoid_json: People with known allergy to ragweed-family plants.
People with gallbladder disease, bile duct obstruction, or kidney disease unless medically supervised.
People taking diuretics, lithium, or complex medication regimens without clinical guidance.
People who are pregnant or nursing unless advised by a qualified professional.
Children unless directed by a healthcare provider.
faq_subtitle: Common questions about dandelion root, leaf, and supplement use.

faq_json.question: What is dandelion used for in supplements?
faq_json.answer: Dandelion is commonly used in digestive, herbal tea, fluid-balance, and liver-support supplement formulas.

faq_json.question: Is dandelion root the same as dandelion leaf?
faq_json.answer: No. Root and leaf have different traditional uses and should be compared separately on labels.

faq_json.question: What is the scientific name for dandelion?
faq_json.answer: The common supplement species is Taraxacum officinale.

faq_json.question: Can dandelion support digestion?
faq_json.answer: Dandelion root is traditionally used as a bitter botanical in digestive wellness routines.

faq_json.question: Does dandelion act like a diuretic?
faq_json.answer: Dandelion leaf is commonly positioned around fluid balance, but people using diuretics should consult a healthcare professional.

faq_json.question: Can I take dandelion with medication?
faq_json.answer: Medication interactions are possible, especially with diuretics, lithium, and blood pressure medications. Professional guidance is recommended.

faq_json.question: Who should avoid dandelion?
faq_json.answer: People with ragweed-family allergies, gallbladder concerns, kidney disease, or complex medication routines should avoid unsupervised use.

faq_json.question: Is dandelion safe during pregnancy?
faq_json.answer: Pregnant or nursing individuals should ask a healthcare professional before using dandelion supplements.

faq_json.question: What should I look for on a dandelion label?
faq_json.answer: Look for plant part, serving size, extract ratio if provided, and whether the formula combines dandelion with other active botanicals.

faq_json.question: Can dandelion replace medical care?
faq_json.answer: No. Dandelion is not a substitute for medical diagnosis, prescribed treatment, or professional care.

sidebar_profile_content: A traditional botanical used as root, leaf, or whole-plant extract in digestive, tea, and wellness formulas.

sidebar_at_a_glance_content: Dandelion labels should clearly identify root, leaf, or whole plant because each format is positioned differently.
full_description: Dandelion is a traditional botanical used in digestive, herbal tea, fluid-balance, and liver-support supplement formulas.
best_for: Digestive wellness, herbal tea routines, and liver-support formulas
safety_level: Use caution with allergies, diuretics, kidney concerns, and gallbladder concerns
